questions.question: Why are retry budgets useful?
options: They cap how much extra work retries can create, so reliability tactics stay within real capacity limits.
They guarantee every request succeeds on a retry.
They make idempotency optional for write paths.
They remove the need to measure dependency saturation.
questions.explanation: Budgets turn speculative reliability behavior into an explicit capacity decision.

questions.question: What is a hedged request trying to improve?
options: Tail latency by sending a second attempt after an initial delay when the first attempt looks unusually slow.
Storage durability by replicating every write twice.
Observability by doubling trace volume.
Security by encrypting the same request on two channels.
questions.explanation: Hedging is a tail-latency technique, not a durability primitive.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to delay a hedge until a percentile threshold instead of sending it immediately?
options: It targets only true tail outliers and avoids spending speculative load on normal fast requests.
It guarantees that no losing request will need cancellation.
It removes the need for independent replicas.
It makes budget accounting unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Delayed hedging tries to help the tail without doubling all traffic.

questions.question: Which trade-off comes with explicit hedge and retry budgets?
options: You bound extra load, but policy tuning and accounting become more complex.
You eliminate every need for performance debugging.
You guarantee that p99 latency will match median latency.
You make cancellation support irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Budgets improve safety at the price of extra operational policy.

questions.question: Why is cancellation of losing hedged requests so important?
options: Without real cancellation, the backend still does duplicate work even after one request already won.
It ensures every request is processed in strict order.
It turns all replicas into one logical node.
It prevents the need for tracing in read paths.
questions.explanation: If losing attempts keep running, the latency gain still comes with most of the load cost.

questions.question: Which metric best tells you whether hedging is worth it?
options: Hedge win rate compared with hedge budget burn and backend saturation.
Total number of Grafana users in the organization.
The count of DNS zones managed by the company.
The mean age of old on-call runbooks.
questions.explanation: A technique is only worth it if the latency benefit justifies the speculative load it spends.

questions.question: When should a request usually not be hedged?
options: When it is non-idempotent or when alternate replicas are highly correlated and unlikely to help.
When it is a read served from independent replicas.
When the service has plenty of spare capacity.
When the client can trace multiple spans.
questions.explanation: Unsafe semantics or correlated replicas make hedging far less attractive.

questions.question: What weakens an answer about hedging most?
options: Talking about faster tails without explaining budgets, cancellation, or replica independence.
Mentioning that speculative work should be measured separately from primary traffic.
Explaining that broad incidents may require disabling hedges.
Calling out read-heavy latency-sensitive paths as the best fit.
questions.explanation: The safety envelope matters as much as the speedup.
